text:
This paper explores the development of social competence among high school students within the framework of non-formal education. The study involved a research experiment with 647 students across seven secondary schools in the Ternopil and Ivano-Frankivsk regions of Ukraine. During the pre-experimental phase, diagnostic methods were aligned with the core structural components of social competence, establishing clear criteria, indicators, and proficiency levels. Initial findings revealed a significant deficit in the social competence of prospective graduates.

To address this, specific socio-pedagogical conditions for non-formal education were implemented from September 2017 to June 2019. These interventions focused on fostering student agency, providing dedicated socio-pedagogical support and monitoring, modernizing educational content and methods, and creating spaces for student-led initiatives. The diverse forms of non-formal education introduced led to a measurable positive shift in the social competence of the experimental groups. Post-experimental results indicated a 15% increase in students achieving a high level of social competence and an 11% reduction in those at the low level. These findings validate the research hypothesis, demonstrating that shifting educational focus toward non-formal models significantly enhances the development of social competence in high school students.
